title: Globally Important Agricultural Heritage Systems (GIAHS)
focus: Concept of GIAHS, its genesis, purpose, and the specific recognized sites in India.
rationale: GIAHS is a distinct international agricultural framework with specific Indian sites that are highly examinable for UPSC prelims, especially for matching sites to their respective states.
sectionExcerpt: GIAHS are resilient agroecosystems characterized by remarkable agrobiodiversity, traditional knowledge, invaluable cultures, and beautiful landscapes. Launched at the World Summit for Sustainable Development in 2002, the programme protects family farming and traditional systems. There are 95 sites globally, including three in India: Saffron Heritage of Kashmir, Koraput Traditional Agriculture in Odisha, and Kuttanad Below Sea Level Farming System in Kerala.
evidenceBullets: Launched at the World Summit for Sustainable Development in 2002.
Protects traditional agricultural systems from climate change, community displacements, and biodiversity loss.
India has three GIAHS sites: Saffron Heritage of Kashmir, Koraput Traditional Agriculture (Odisha), and Kuttanad Below Sea Level Farming System (Kerala).

title: Agroforestry and Model Rules 2025
focus: Model Rules 2025 for tree felling, agroforestry systems in India, and related government initiatives.
rationale: Agroforestry is a key component of sustainable agriculture. The new Model Rules 2025, various agroforestry systems, and India's Bonn Challenge pledge form a solid cluster for policy and conceptual questions.
sectionExcerpt: The Government issued Model Rules 2025 for felling trees on agricultural land, overseen by State Level Committees. Applicants register in the National Timber Management System. Agroforestry occupies approximately 8% of India's land. Systems include Agrisilviculture (crops and trees), Agri-horticulture, and Silvi-pasture. Initiatives include the National Agroforestry Policy 2014, SubMission on Agroforestry, and India's Bonn Challenge pledge to restore 13 million hectares of degraded land by 2020.
evidenceBullets: Model Rules 2025 require Felling Permits for agricultural lands with more than 10 trees, and NOCs for 10 or fewer trees.
Applicants must register in the National Timber Management System with land ownership details.
Agroforestry occupies approximately 8% of India's geographical land area.
India joined the Bonn Challenge to restore 13 million hectares of degraded and deforested land by 2020.
Agroforestry systems include Agrisilviculture (combines crops with tree crops) and Silvi-pasture (integrates livestock, forage, and trees).

title: Environment Audit (EA) Rules, 2025
focus: The framework, tiers, and statutory backing of the newly notified Environment Audit Rules, 2025.
rationale: New environmental rules are prime targets for UPSC. The distinction between EA and EIA, the two-tiered system, and the statutory origin under the EPA 1986 are highly testable facts.
sectionExcerpt: MoEFCC notified the Environment Audit (EA) Rules, 2025 under the Environment Protection Act (1986). EA differs from EIA as it determines conformity to an approved Environmental Management Plan post-establishment. It features a Two-Tiered System: Tier-1 (government regulator review by CPCB/SPCB) and Tier-2 (Environment auditor-based). An Environment Audit Designate Agency (EADA) oversees certification and registration of auditors.
evidenceBullets: EA Rules 2025 were developed under the Environment Protection Act (1986).
EA differs from EIA as EIA is conducted prior to project establishment, while EA checks conformity to the Environmental Management Plan.
Introduced initially in 1992 under the Environment (Protection) Rules, requiring annual environmental statements to SPCBs.
Features a Two-Tiered System: Tier-1 (CPCB/SPCB review) and Tier-2 (Environment auditor-based mechanism).
Environment Audit Designate Agency (EADA) is responsible for certification, registration, and oversight of Environment Auditors.

title: Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) and Ex-Post Facto Clearance
focus: Supreme Court rulings on ex-post facto environmental clearance for mining projects.
rationale: Judicial interventions in environmental governance, such as rulings on ex-post facto EC, are frequently tested in UPSC Polity and Environment sections.
sectionExcerpt: The Supreme Court reviewed and overturned its May 2025 decision in Vanashakti v. Union of India, which had restrained the Centre from granting ex-post facto environmental clearance (EC) to mining projects or regularising actions contravening the 2006 EIA notification. An ex-post facto EC allows a project proponent to continue without obtaining an EC prior to project commencement.
evidenceBullets: The Supreme Court overturned its decision that restrained the Centre from granting ex-post facto environmental clearance to mining projects.
Ex-post facto EC allows a project to continue without having obtained an Environmental Clearance prior to its commencement.
